title: Relative Pronouns (who - when - where - which - whose)
title_en: Relative Pronouns (who - when - where - which - whose)
objectives: Apply suitable relative pronouns to connect ideas when completing written tasks about real-life qualities and situations
Appreciate classmates' ideas by listening respectfully when they share their written clues using relative clauses

key_concepts.explanation: We use relative pronouns to connect ideas in one sentence. Five relative pronouns: who (for people), when (for time), where (for place), which (for things/ideas), whose (for belonging/possession).

who → people: The nurse who helps children is very kind. when → time: Evening is the time when I sit with my family. where → place: This is the room where we eat lunch. which → things/ideas: This is the book which tells my favourite story. whose → belonging: She is the girl whose project won the prize.
Whose already shows belonging. So when you join two sentences with whose, do not use another word for belonging like his/her/their/my after it.

examples.problem: A friend ___ helps others is thoughtful.
examples.answer: A friend who helps others is thoughtful.

examples.problem: This is the bag ___ I use every day.
examples.answer: This is the bag which I use every day.

examples.problem: The café ___ we study is quiet.
examples.answer: The café where we study is quiet.

exercises.problem: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: 1. I know a classmate ___ always arrives on time. 2. Saturday is the day ___ we visit our grandparents. 3. This is the pen ___ I use for my notes. 4. He is the boy ___ backpack is always organised.

Connect using the correct relative pronoun: 1. I remember a morning. We left home very early. 2. My sister has a friend. Her drawings are amazing. 3. This is the park. We play football there at weekends.
exercises.answer: 1. I remember a morning when we left home very early. 2. My sister has a friend whose drawings are amazing. 3. This is the park where we play football at weekends.

exercises.problem: The Detective Clues: Imagine you are a young detective. Write three short sentences using relative pronouns to describe what you saw. Choose from: I saw a person ___, I stopped at a place ___, I remember a moment ___, I met someone ___. Share with two classmates.
exercises.answer: Example: 'I met a man who lost his wallet near the park where I usually walk.'

teaching_notes: Grammar lesson on 5 relative pronouns: who, when, where, which, whose. Grammar Box table with pronoun, usage, and example. Think-Pair-Share with model sentences. Tip about 'whose' not needing extra possessive word. Practice: fill-in → combine sentences → creative detective writing. 1 page.
